coredata.dc:title: Laser induced generation of hydrogen by using NdAlO3 nanocrystals as photocatalysts in alcohols
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gonçalves, J.M.
Miśta, W.
Wiewiórski, P.
Stefanski, M.
Tomala, R.
Strek, W.
coredata.dc:description: Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) using focused infrared laser beam can emit hot electrons and, for this reason, has been described as capable of generating H2. NdAlO3 is described as a photocatalyst for H2 production in several alcohols using a focused 808 nm excitation; the process generated a low concentration of CH4 and no presence of carbon dots in the final solution, improving previous results using graphene foam. The proposed mechanism involves the ejection of hot electrons from the material and their subsequent absorption by the alcohol, ultimately leading to dehydrogenation. Besides that, relative permittivity was correlated with the amount of electrons emitted, which was demonstrated by a superb correlation between H2 production and relative permittivity of the media used. Higher relative permittivity media was able to emit more hot electrons, which ultimately produced more H2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Hot electrons
White emission
Perovskite
Nanocrystals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs (a–b) and diffuse reflectance spectrum of NdAlO3.

Digital photographs of LIWE phenomenon from NdAlO3 pellet in air using 2.0 W laser excitation power (a) and 3.3 W excitation power (b). Emission spectrum with 3.3 W excitation power in the different alcohols (c).
Digital photographs of simultaneous LIWE and gas bubbles evolving from the surface of the NdAlO3 pellet in methanol (a–b).
The rate of H2 evolution (in μmol/h) as a function of the number of carbon atoms contained in the alcohol (a) and the relative permittivity (b).

Schematic representation of the proposed mechanism for decomposing alcohols into H2. The first step is the absorption of the focused 808 nm excitation, which emits hot electrons that are captured by the alcohol in the second step, ultimately dehydrogenates and produces H2. High relative permittivity medium (on the left) produces more hot electrons and, therefore more H2 than low permittivity medium (on the right).
Amount of gas products generated by the photocatalytic reaction a .

head.ce:title: Laser induced generation of hydrogen by using NdAlO3 nanocrystals as photocatalysts in alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Low Temperature and Structure Research, Polish Academy of Sciences, 50-422 Wroclaw, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Low Temperature and Structure Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Low Temperature and Structure Research, Polish Academy of Sciences, 50-422 Wroclaw, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) using focused infrared laser beam can emit hot electrons and, for this reason, has been described as capable of generating H2. NdAlO3 is described as a photocatalyst for H2 production in several alcohols using a focused 808 nm excitation; the process generated a low concentration of CH4 and no presence of carbon dots in the final solution, improving previous results using graphene foam. The proposed mechanism involves the ejection of hot electrons from the material and their subsequent absorption by the alcohol, ultimately leading to dehydrogenation. Besides that, relative permittivity was correlated with the amount of electrons emitted, which was demonstrated by a superb correlation between H2 production and relative permittivity of the media used. Higher relative permittivity media was able to emit more hot electrons, which ultimately produced more H2.

ce:section.ce:para: Most recent United Nations projections expect human population growth to reach 10 billion in 2050 and around 12 billion in 2100 [1]; as a result, energy consumption will also rise, with the U.S. Energy Information Administration projecting fossil fuel production and usage to grow still [2]. The environmental impact of these non-renewable energy sources has been increasing not only for their limited availability, but also for their polluting emissions contributing to climate change [3,4]. In this context, hydrogen gas (H2) is considered as an alternative to fossil fuels.

H2 is regarded as an energy carrier, since it needs to be produced from another source to be able to produce energy [5]. Although most of the hydrogen produced today is from steam reforming of natural gas [6], which not only uses non-renewable sources, but also contributes to high emissions of CO2 [7]. New green hydrogen production methods from renewable sources, use biomass and biological processes (for example biophotolysis of water using algae and cyanobacteria) [7–11], wind power [12–14] or solar energy [15,16]. Additionally, fuel cells that generate electrical energy using hydrogen and internal combustion engines produce no hazardous byproducts [17,18]. Therefore, hydrogen can be considered a green energy source.

Photocatalytic generation of H2 has been widely explored in the literature. TiO2 has been studied since 1972 as a photocatalyst to produce hydrogen through water [19] and methanol [20]. More recently, TiO2 was also modified with cocatalysts, such as Pt [21] or CuS [22] to improve H2 production. However, they were only explored using ultraviolet radiation. Lanthanide doped TiO2 operating in the visible range were studied as photocatalysts, but mainly for organic molecule degradation [23–26]. However other lanthanide-containing materials have been described as catalysts for hydrogen production [27,28].

The vast majority of photocatalysts operate in the ultraviolet or visible region. To further expand the spectral region into the infrared and harvest low energy photons to improve photocatalysis, a phenomenon known as Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) can be used: this type of emission is characterized by a broadband warm white emission which can be observed in several different materials [29–34] and uses an infrared focused laser beam as excitation source. One of the mechanisms proposed for this emission is a multiphoton ionization [35] and due to the high excitation power density obtained through focusing the light, electrons can be ejected from the material with high kinetic energy [36], called hot electrons, that can be ultimately used for catalysis.

These hot electrons can assist catalytic processes by populating previously unoccupied orbitals of adsorbed species inducing electron-mediated dissociation [37]. In overall water splitting for instance the reaction is thermodynamically unfavored, needing an activation energy of 237 kJ/mol [38], thus hot electron-driven catalysis might be a feasible option. Additionally, their production by surface plasmon resonance in plasmonic structures was linked to the hydrolysis of ammonia borane [39] and ethylene epoxidation [40]. Recently, hot electrons were reported to assist in catalysis in two different ways: being transferred to the LUMO of O2 molecules weakening their bond and producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) responsible for reactions and also contribute to photothermal reactions due to the thermalization of their kinetic energy [41].

LIWE was also used for H2 production [42–44]. However, along with the desired gas production (H2), a high amount of methane (CH4) was also observed, together with the formation of carbon dots, which may hinder long time H2 generation. One way to overcome such limitations would be using inorganic materials, such as aluminates. Herein, we describe a new lanthanide-containing photocatalyst to produce hydrogen gas using a focused infrared excitation source, with low CH4 and no carbon dot generation. Furthermore, the involvement of hot electrons emission in the catalysis was accessed, and the amount of electrons emitted was connected to the relative permittivity of the liquid alcohol surrounding the sample.

NdAlO3 nanocrystals were obtained using Pechini sol-gel method. In short, Nd2O3 was dissolved in diluted HNO3 and recrystallized three times in order to obtain its respective nitrate. Subsequently, a stoichiometric amount of AlCl3 and a 5-times excess of citric acid in relation to the metals were added to the nitrate and left stirring for 30 min. After that, ethylene glycol (1:1 M proportion to citric acid) was added with continuous stirring for 1 h. After drying at 90 °C for 48 h, the transparent sol turned into a resin that was later calcinated at 900 °C for 16 h in a muffle furnace. An Agilent CARY 5000 UV–Vis–NIR equipped with an integrating sphere was used to obtain the diffuse reflectance spectrum. Transmission Electron Microscopy (Philips CM-20 SuperTwin microscope operating at 160 kV) was performed to examine the sample's morphology. The Powder X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) was performed in a PANalitycal X'Pert Pro diffractometer with a Cu radiation source.

The powders were pressed in the form of pellets using a hydraulic press (25 kPa) and immersed in methanol (99.8%, POCH), ethanol (99.9%, Stanlab), 2-propanol (99.7%, POCH), 1-butanol (pure, p.a., Chempur). Infrared excitation was achieved with a focused 808 nm laser diode (maximum power = 3.3 W). The yield of H2 was determined by using an online gas chromatograph (GC) equipped with 13X molecular sieves column and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD). Ar was used as the carrier gas (5 mL/min flow) with 1 mL gas loops. Additionally, gaseous products were monitored with an OmniStar QMS-200 mass spectrometer (Pfeiffer Vacuum) calibrated with gas mixtures of known composition.

The XRD of the synthesized sample is presented in Fig. S1. Pure trigonal perovskite phase of NdAlO3 (#COD: 1521178) was obtained and by using Scherrer's equation [45,46] on the highest intensity diffraction peak, the crystallite size calculated was 35 nm, which renders them the quality of nanocrystals. TEM images show that the material was composed of aggregated particles with sizes around 35 nm (Fig. 1 a–b), corroborating the calculated from the XRD. Additionally, the d-spacing in Fig. 1b was 0.36 nm, indicating the (102) plane. The diffuse reflectance spectrum (Fig. 1c) showed several visible and near-infrared transitions typical from Nd3+ [47] and the narrow line structure is typical from forbidden intra-f transitions [48] Moreover, an intense absorption at 808 nm was observed and further used as excitation for LIWE.

By exciting the sample with a focused 808 nm laser in ambient pressure, an intense white emission was observed (Fig. 2 ). This phenomenon has been described in the literature as Laser Induced White Emission (LIWE) [29–34]. One of its characteristics is hot electrons emission [36], which might responsible for hydrogen generation in graphene-based materials for methanol [42], ethanol [43] and water [44]. This prompted the investigation of hydrogen generation in alcohols using NdAlO3. In all alcohols the emission spectra was very similar, consisted of a very broad band comprising the whole visible range (Fig. 2c), which is in accordance with the literature [42–44]. It is important to highlight that 808 nm was the chosen excitation wavelength due to Nd3+ known absorption in this region as also observed in Fig. 1c.

A secondary characteristic observed was the evolution of gas bubbles at the sample's surface, as can be seen in Fig. 3 . When the bubbles were attached to the surface of the NdAlO3 pellet, the intensity of LIWE decreased due to changes in the focus of the excitation source. When bubbles were released from the surface, LIWE was very intense. This phenomenon caused the emission of white light to be intermittent, as seen in Supplementary Materials Videos 1-4, each video representing the emission in a different alcohol.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2023.03.144

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article.
Video 1 Video 1
Video 2 Video 2
Video 3 Video 3
Video 4 Video 4

To analyze the composition of the gas bubbles, the experiment was performed connected to a mass spectrometer: Laser excitation power was fixed at 3.3 W and argon was used as a carrier gas in order to conduct the gaseous products of the photocatalytic reaction into the mass spectrometer. At lower excitation power, no hydrogen production was observed due to the absence of hot electrons emission; therefore, the catalytic reaction was not initiated.

The products of the reaction were H2, CH4 and CO2 gases with a significant fraction of hydrogen. Fig. 4 a shows the rate of H2 evolution in μmol/h as a function of the number of carbon atoms in the alcohol. It demonstrates an exponential decrease, meaning hydrogen production highly depends on the experiment's medium.

This lower production of H2 with increasing the number of carbon atoms might be associated with relative permittivity of the alcohols, as shown in Fig. 4b. Since LIWE is related to the ejection of hot electrons [36], it is hypothesized that in solvents with higher relative permittivity, the electrons are more easily ejected from the sample, which in turn can decompose the alcohols into H2 and other gases.

Table 1 shows all gas products generated by the photocatalytic reaction. It can be seen that H2 is the more abundant product, as mentioned previously. The highest hydrogen production was obtained in methanol, yet it decreased steadily as the carbon chain length increased.

The results can be compared to other already described in the literature, where graphene foam was used as LIWE photocatalyst [42]. In methanol, NdAlO3 was able to produce slightly more H2 (57.79% in graphene in comparison to 58.70% in NdAlO3), however, the methane production was considerably lower (20.04% in graphene and 5.34% in NdAlO3). Taking into consideration that CH4 is a toxic gas affecting environment and human health, such result is a considerable improvement. Furthermore, infrared laser induced hydrogen production was highly dependent of laser power, as previously reported [42–44]. Since the hydrogen evolution from the photocatalyst was not observed before the maximum power of the available laser was reached, it is reasonable to expect that the performance can be further improved by raising the excitation power.

Several different mechanisms were considered. The first one was a pure thermal decomposition of methanol, that occurs between 650 °C and 750 °C and produce solely CO and H2 [49]. Although high temperatures are observed in LIWE (in the order of 630 °C [30,50–52]), two factors dismissed this mechanism: the temperature reached might be too low, coupled with the detection of CH4 and CO2. This indicate that other mechanisms should be considered. Additionally, the temperature of the system was evaluated by inserting a thermocouple distanced by approximately 5 mm from the sample, which showed that the temperature did not reach 50 °C in any sample as shown in Fig. S2, showcasing that temperature increase was only confined to the excitation laser spot area and the alcohol decomposition was in fact taking place only at the surface of the excited sample.

A photo-assisted thermal decomposition of methanol involves dehydration and dehydrogenation, which could explain all the products [53]. However, in 2-propanol this proposition led to the detection of propene and acetone [53,54] not observed in this case. Additionally, when comparing the rate of H2 production to C–OH, C–H and O–H bond dissociation energies no correlation was found (Fig. S3), leading to the conclusion that a simple bond cleavage of adsorbed species might not be involved.

Since hot electrons are known to assist in photocatalysis, we propose a mechanism involving their emission (Fig. 5 ) based on previous observations [43,55]. The first step is the multiphoton ionization of NdAlO3 (1) due to the high excitation power density of the focused laser beam; this step is responsible for the emission of hot electrons. The second step is the transfer of these electrons to the alcohol surrounding the sample which will ultimately decompose into a mixture of gases with H2 being the primary product (2). Additionally hot electrons might also thermally lose their kinetic energy leading to a local temperature increase [41]. This fact cannot be disregarded in playing a secondary role in further decomposing the alcohol or intermediate products.
(1)
N d A l
O 3
+ N h ν
→ N d A l
O 3
+
+
e −
(2)
2
H − R − O H +
4 e
−
→
2
R −
O H
−
+ 2
H −
→ 2
R = O +
3 / 2
H 2
+ 4
e −
where hν represent the incident photons, e− hot electrons and R the side chain of the alcohol.

To probe hot electrons emission, Park and collaborators used Au/TiO2 electrodes modified with Pt nanoparticles during CO oxidation, and the generation of chemicurrent was attributed to hot electrons [55]. NdAlO3 has been previously studied in LIWE [33] and more recently the Nd3+ concentration in LaAlO3 was reported [56]. Photoconductivity measurements showed a decrease in resistivity of 4 orders of magnitude in NdAlO3 attributed to the creation of charges in the sample. Additionally, generation of current in the order of 10 μA was also measured. This can be regarded as evidence of the emission of hot electrons that initiate the photocatalyst reactions.

The influence of the relative permittivity of the liquid alcohol is related to the LIWE phenomenon. Since light emission is closely related to the emission of hot electrons, it is assumed that a high relative permittivity medium (such as methanol) facilitates the ejection of high kinetic energy electrons. By changing the medium and maintaining the excitation power constant, the amount of hot electrons to be used in catalytic reaction produced by low permittivity media is lower, decreasing the amount of generated H2.

NdAlO3 perovskite nanocrystals were successfully synthesized in pure cubic phase with no impurities detected in XRD. This material demonstrated intense warm white emission in ambient pressure through focused infrared laser excitation, which was also seen when immersed in different alcohols. The emission was accompanied by gas bubbles evolving from the pellet's surface made from NdAlO3 nanocrystals, causing the emission to be intermittent. These bubbles' composition was analyzed by mass spectrometry showing that the main product was H2 with a small amount of other gases, with the highest production of hydrogen at about 600 μmol/h in methanol. Compared to graphene used a as photocatalyst target using the same principle, NdAlO3 perovskites showed the advantage of producing a low amount of CH4 and no presence of carbon dots after laser irradiation. An excellent correlation of the amount of H2 generated with the relative permittivity of the alcohol indicates that the ejection of hot electrons is involved. The proposed mechanism for production is the emission of hot electrons from the surface of NdAlO3, which are ultimately absorbed by the alcohol leading to their dehydrogenation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge National Science Centre of Poland for the financial support (Grant No. NCN-2020/37/B/ST5/02399).
